result:
The rain came down in sheets, the kind that turned the city into a watercolor smear of neon and regret. I stood under the awning of a closed laundromat on 14th and watched the droplets chase each other down the glass like they had somewhere better to be. My coat was soaked through. My cigarettes were soaked through. Even my thoughts felt waterlogged.

There’s something quietly magnificent about being completely unprepared for the weather. It strips away the illusion that we’re in control. One minute you’re a functioning adult with opinions about podcast hosts; the next you’re a soggy primate trying to remember which way the bus stop is. I like that about rain. It’s democratic. It ruins everyone’s plans with the same indifferent grace.

I started walking anyway. The city at 1:17 a.m. has its own dialect. The hiss of tires on wet asphalt. The distant argument between a man and his own bad decisions echoing from a sixth-floor window. The low mechanical heartbeat of the subway breathing beneath the pavement. If you listen long enough, you start to hear the rhythm underneath it all—like the city is humming to itself, half lullaby, half threat.

I’ve been thinking lately about how we build our lives like nesting dolls. There’s the version we show our parents, smaller and shinier. Inside that is the version we show our friends, a little more honest but still curated. Deeper still is the person we are with our lover at 3 a.m. when neither of us can sleep. And at the very center, so small it’s almost invisible, is the creature we actually are. The one that’s afraid of the dark and wants to be held and occasionally wonders if we’re secretly a disappointment to the universe.

Most of us spend our entire lives trying not to look directly at that smallest doll.

A cab blasts past, spraying me with gutter water. I laugh because what else can you do? The universe has a sense of humor. It’s just profoundly stupid.

I remember once, years ago, standing on a bridge in Prague at sunrise. The Charles Bridge was empty except for me and a pair of very serious-looking ravens. The city was still asleep, wrapped in that particular pink-gold light that makes even communists look romantic. I had been traveling for weeks with almost no money, sleeping in night trains and eating bread and mustard packets stolen from cafés. I was twenty-three and convinced I was either going to become a famous writer or die dramatically in some European alley. Both outcomes seemed equally plausible and appealing.

One of the ravens looked at me with what I can only describe as avian contempt. Then it shit on the bridge inches from my shoe. I took this as a sign. Not from God—God was busy—but from something. Maybe the universe was telling me that no matter how beautiful the moment, there will always be shit involved. The trick is to stop being surprised by it.

I’ve carried that philosophy with me ever since. Marriage, career changes, two different continents, one spectacular public failure that still makes me wince when I think about it at 4 a.m.—all of it has contained both transcendence and pigeon shit in various ratios. The people who can’t handle that ratio tend to become either bitter or insufferable on the internet. The ones who can usually end up with decent lives and the occasional really good story.

The rain is letting up now. Just a drizzle, the kind that feels like the sky is exhaling after holding its breath too long. I pass a twenty-four-hour diner where a waitress is slowly filling salt shakers like she’s performing a ritual. There’s an old man at the counter who looks like he’s been there since the Carter administration. He’s reading a newspaper with the focused intensity of someone who still believes facts matter. I love him immediately.

Inside, the air is thick with coffee and bacon and the particular loneliness that only exists in places open at this hour. I order coffee and a slice of apple pie that tastes like it was baked by someone who understood sorrow. The old man catches my eye and nods once, the universal greeting of night people. We are members of the same strange fraternity—the ones who orbit the edges of other people’s daylight hours.

I think about how much of life is just showing up in rooms where other people are also showing up. How revolutionary that is, really. We’re all just animals who learned to wear pants and feel existential dread, trying to make each other feel slightly less alone before the heat death of the universe. Sometimes we succeed. Usually we order pie.

My phone buzzes. A text from my friend Marcus who never sleeps: “You awake?” I reply with a photo of the pie. He sends back a picture of his cat looking profoundly disappointed in humanity. This is how we say “I see you” in the 21st century.

The thing nobody tells you about getting older is that your references start to pile up like unread books. Every new person you meet requires a small archaeological dig through your own history just to have a conversation. “Remember when” becomes a full-time occupation. But there’s a strange comfort in it too. Your past starts to feel less like a burden and more like an old dog that follows you everywhere, occasionally knocking things over but mostly just happy to be near you.

I finish the pie. The waitress refills my coffee without asking. This is love, I think. Not grand gestures but the quiet assumption that you’ll want more coffee.

Outside, the rain has stopped completely. The streets shine like they’ve been varnished. The city looks newborn. I walk toward the river because that’s what you do when the night feels like this—like it’s offering you something if you’re brave enough to accept it.

The water is black and moving with mysterious purpose. Across the river, the lights of the financial district flicker like dying stars. All that money. All that ambition. All those people who think winning is the point. I feel a sudden flood of tenderness for every single one of them. We’re all just trying to feel less temporary.

A memory surfaces, unbidden: my grandmother teaching me to make bread when I was seven. The way she’d slap the dough with her strong hands, talking to it like it was a difficult but beloved child. “You have to be firm but kind,” she’d say. “Bread remembers.” I think about that a lot now. How everything remembers. How we leave traces of ourselves in places we’ll never return to. In the way we close doors. In the silences between sentences. In half-finished poems and unanswered texts and the particular way we say someone’s name when we’re tired.

I don’t know what I’m trying to say anymore. Maybe that’s the point. The rain, the pie, the old man, the river—they don’t add up to anything neat. Life doesn’t resolve. It just keeps offering itself to you, moment after ridiculous moment, asking only that you pay attention.

I light a cigarette that somehow survived the rain. It tastes like hope and poor decisions. Perfect.

Somewhere, a siren wails. A couple laughs too loud on the bridge above me. The river keeps moving, carrying God knows what toward the sea. And I stand there, thirty-eight years old, soaked and caffeinated and strangely, stupidly alive, feeling the small creature at my center stretch and yawn and look around with something that might be wonder.

The night is almost over. Soon the sun will come up and ruin everything with its optimism. People will start performing competence again. But for now, in this small pocket of darkness between yesterday and tomorrow, the world feels exactly as large and as small as it needs to be.

I take another drag. The smoke curls up like a question mark.

I don’t answer it.

There’s no need.

(Word count: 1,012)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
